title: Getting random 403 Forbidden errors on the dashboard
body: I keep getting 403 errors when I try to access the TaskFlow dashboard. It's not every time — sometimes it loads fine, sometimes I get a 403 page. It seems totally random. My permissions haven't changed and other people on my team say they're seeing it too. Started happening a couple days ago.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for reporting this — intermittent 403s affecting multiple people definitely sounds like something changed on the backend rather than a permissions issue on your end.

To start narrowing this down: were there any deployments, infrastructure changes, or updates to your authentication/SSO provider around the time this started (a couple days ago)? And when you hit the 403, is it on the initial dashboard load, or does it happen on specific actions within the dashboard (e.g., loading a particular project, filtering tasks, etc.)?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I'm not sure about deployments — I'm not on the infrastructure team so I don't really have visibility into that. I do know my role was recently changed to the new 'analyst' role, but I don't think that's related since it was working fine for a bit after that. As for the 403, it happens right on the initial page load. Like I'll just navigate to the dashboard and sometimes it loads, sometimes 403. If I refresh a couple times it usually comes back. I've been assuming it's some kind of network issue on our end.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That role change to 'analyst' is actually a very promising lead here — even though it worked fine for a bit afterward, intermittent 403s can point to a caching or propagation issue with role definitions rather than your individual permissions.

Two things would help narrow this down:

1. **The other teammates seeing this** — are they also on the 'analyst' role, or do they have different roles? This would tell us whether it's role-specific.
2. **Timing:** You said the role change happened and things worked fine "for a bit" before the 403s started. Can you estimate how long that gap was? (Hours? A day or two? A week?)

This will help us figure out whether the 403s correlate with the analyst role itself or with something else that changed around the same time.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Good question — yeah, now that I think about it, the teammates who mentioned seeing it are also on the analyst role. I asked one of our admins and she said she hasn't had any issues at all. So maybe it is related to the role? As for the timing, I think I got the analyst role maybe a few days before the 403s started? It wasn't immediate — it worked fine for at least a day or so, then the errors started showing up. I'd say the 403s have been happening for about two days now.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
The pattern is looking pretty clear: this is specific to the 'analyst' role, and the intermittent nature suggests some kind of caching is involved. Here's my working theory — the 'analyst' role's permissions for the dashboard may not be consistently resolved, possibly due to a permissions cache or session state that sometimes evaluates correctly and sometimes doesn't.

To confirm this, could you check one thing next time you hit the 403: **what does the 403 page actually look like?** Specifically:
- Is it TaskFlow's own error page (branded, with your app's styling), or is it a generic/plain 403 page (which would suggest a load balancer or reverse proxy is blocking you before the request even reaches the app)?
- If you can open your browser's developer tools (Network tab) before refreshing, the response headers on that 403 would be very helpful — particularly any `Server`, `X-Cache`, or `Via` headers.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
It's TaskFlow's own error page — it has the logo and the normal app styling, just says something like 'You don't have permission to access this resource.' So it's not a generic browser error or anything like that.

I'm not super familiar with the dev tools stuff but I did try opening the Network tab like you said. I didn't see anything obvious for X-Cache or Via headers. The Server header just showed the normal app server info I think. I can try again next time it happens and screenshot it if that would help?

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Intermittent 403 on dashboard for users with the new 'analyst' role — likely permissions cache inconsistency","problem":"Users assigned the recently-created 'analyst' role are intermittently receiving 403 Forbidden errors when loading the TaskFlow dashboard. The errors come from the application's own authorization layer (branded error page). Refreshing a few times usually resolves it temporarily. Admin-role users are not affected. The issue began approximately one day after the analyst role was assigned, and has persisted for about two days.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The application's permission/authorization cache is inconsistently resolving the 'analyst' role's access to the dashboard resource. Most likely scenario: the analyst role's dashboard permission is either (a) not fully propagated across all app server instances or cache nodes, or (b) subject to a cache eviction/rebuild race where stale entries intermittently deny access. The one-day delay between role assignment and symptom onset is consistent with a cache TTL expiring and the role's permissions not being correctly re-cached on every rebuild cycle.","reproduction_steps":["Assign a user the 'analyst' role in TaskFlow","Wait approximately 24 hours (or until the permissions cache cycles)","Navigate to the TaskFlow dashboard repeatedly","Observe that some loads return 403 ('You don't have permission to access this resource') while others succeed","Confirm that switching the same user to an admin role eliminates the 403s"],"environment":"TaskFlow production environment. Affects all users with the new 'analyst' role. No specific browser, OS, or network dependency identified — the issue is server-side.","severity":"high","impact":"All users assigned the 'analyst' role are intermittently locked out of the dashboard, which is likely a primary workspace. Multiple team members are affected. There is a partial workaround (refreshing), but it degrades productivity and trust in the application.","recommended_fix":"1. Inspect the permission/role definitions for the 'analyst' role — verify that dashboard access is explicitly granted and not relying on implicit or inherited permissions that may not resolve consistently. 2. Examine the authorization cache layer (in-memory cache, Redis, etc.) for how role-to-permission mappings are stored and invalidated — look for inconsistencies across app server instances if load-balanced. 3. Check whether the cache rebuild/hydration logic correctly picks up all permissions for newly-created roles vs. legacy roles. 4. As an immediate mitigation, consider clearing the permissions cache or forcing a full re-sync of analyst role permissions across all instances.","proposed_test_case":"Create an integration test that: (1) creates a new role with dashboard access, (2) assigns it to a test user, (3) makes 50+ sequential dashboard requests as that user across different app instances (if applicable), and (4) asserts that zero requests return 403. Additionally, add a test that invalidates the permissions cache mid-sequence and verifies access is maintained after cache rebuild.","information_gaps":["Exact number of app server instances and whether a load balancer distributes requests across them (would explain why some requests succeed and others fail)","The specific caching mechanism used for permission resolution (in-memory, distributed cache, DB query cache)","Whether any deployment or configuration change coincided with the onset — the reporter lacked visibility into this","Exact cache TTL and invalidation strategy for role-permission mappings"]}
